title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendolyn approaches the Erskine residence and overhears a heated argument through the French windows between Colonel Erskine and his wife, Janet. Janet accuses the Colonel of infidelity, specifically naming a blonde woman from Dilworth named Harriet. In her distress, Janet threatens to commit suicide by jumping off a cliff. Gwendolyn retreats to the driveway, asks a servant to move Mrs. Erskine's car, and returns to her hotel to meet Jiles. She informs Jiles that the Colonel was not the man who ran away with Helen and that the Colonel maintains Helen was still alive when he last saw her that night.

Later, while Miss Marple is weeding the garden, Dr. Kennedy arrives to see Mrs. Coke. Miss Marple introduces herself, explaining that she is assisting Gwendolyn with the gardening. Dr. Kennedy appears anxious and exhausted, revealing that Charles Reed has requested letters written by his sister, Helen, to determine if they are forgeries. Dr. Kennedy admits his certainty regarding Kelvin Harley's guilt is wavering; while he previously believed Harley strangled Helen and staged her disappearance with a suitcase and letters, he now finds it difficult to believe Helen would remain silent for years if she were alive, yet equally strange that no one reported her death if she were dead. He leaves a sample of Helen's handwriting—a plant order—and a second letter for the Reeds to examine. Dr. Kennedy expresses fear that if Harley is a murderer, he is a cold-blooded, premeditated killer. Miss Marple concludes the encounter by asking Dr. Kennedy if Helen was afraid of anyone, specifically mentioning a young man named Avril.
